questions.question: When rotating authentication credentials for Temporal Cloud Workers, why is it recommended to support both old and new credentials at the same time during the process?

choices.text: To meet a Temporal Cloud licensing requirement that mandates overlapping credential periods for all namespaces
To allow Workers to be migrated gradually without causing authentication failures that could disrupt running workflows
Because Temporal Cloud automatically deletes old credentials after 24 hours, requiring a brief overlap window
To give the certificate authority time to propagate the new certificate to all regional Temporal Cloud endpoints

questions.explanation: The dual-credential step exists to avoid downtime. If you immediately removed old credentials before all Workers were updated, any Worker still using the old credential would fail to authenticate and could disrupt active workflows. By temporarily supporting both, you can migrate Workers one by one, confirm everything works, and only then remove the old credentials. This is a practical example of Temporal's broader theme: keeping systems running reliably even during maintenance operations.

questions.question: A team notices their Temporal Cloud bill is much higher than expected. They trace the cause to a single Activity that calls an external payment API and retries very frequently whenever the API is temporarily down. What is the most direct reason retries increase cost on Temporal Cloud?

choices.text: Retries cause the entire Workflow to restart from the beginning, which creates a large number of new Workflow Executions
Each Activity retry is counted as a separate billable Action, so a burst of retries during an outage generates many extra charges
Retrying Activities forces Temporal Cloud to spin up additional Worker instances, which are billed as compute resources
Failed Activities are moved to a special high-cost retention tier, increasing storage costs with each retry attempt

questions.explanation: On Temporal Cloud, each Activity execution — including each retry — counts as one billable Action. So if an external service goes down and Temporal retries an Activity dozens of times across thousands of concurrent Workflows, the Action count can spike dramatically. This is why tuning your Retry Policy matters: setting a higher initial retry interval, capping maximum attempts, or marking certain errors as non-retryable can significantly reduce unnecessary Actions and cost without sacrificing reliability.

questions.question: An organization is building an internal knowledge hub to help developers adopt Temporal. According to best practices, what content should the knowledge hub focus on?

choices.text: Complete copies of Temporal's official SDK API references and concept explanations, kept in sync with each Temporal release
Organization-specific decisions, conventions, deployment standards, and operational procedures that Temporal's public documentation does not cover
Only beginner tutorials and quickstarts, since advanced topics should always be handled directly by the Temporal support team
A catalog of every Temporal feature with detailed technical descriptions, so developers never need to visit the official docs

questions.explanation: The knowledge hub's value comes from the things Temporal's public documentation cannot provide: your organization's specific Namespace naming conventions, internal deployment standards, escalation paths, cost guidance for your use cases, and decisions your team has already made. Duplicating official docs creates a maintenance burden — when Temporal updates its docs, your copy becomes outdated and confusing. Link to the official docs for general concepts, and reserve the knowledge hub for context that is unique to your organization.

questions.question: Your organization uses the same internal CA to sign certificates for both dev and prod Temporal Cloud namespaces. A developer is worried that a dev certificate could accidentally be used to connect to the prod namespace. What is the recommended Temporal Cloud mechanism to prevent this?

choices.text: Issue separate API keys for dev and prod namespaces, since mTLS certificates signed by the same CA cannot be scoped to a single namespace under any configuration.
Use Let's Encrypt to generate namespace-specific certificates, since it guarantees that each certificate is bound to a unique domain and cannot be reused across namespaces.
Give each certificate a common name matching its target namespace and configure Certificate Filters on the namespace to reject certificates whose common name does not match.
Create a dedicated CA per environment so that dev and prod certificates are cryptographically unrelated, which is the only approach Temporal Cloud supports for namespace isolation.

questions.explanation: When multiple certificates are signed by the same CA, Certificate Filters provide an additional authorization check at the namespace level. By convention, you give each certificate a Common Name matching the namespace name, then configure the namespace's Certificate Filters to only accept certificates with that common name. This prevents a dev certificate from authenticating to the prod namespace even though both share the same CA. Option A is incorrect because mTLS certificates can be scoped using Certificate Filters without switching to API keys. Option B is wrong because Let's Encrypt is explicitly not recommended for Temporal mTLS — it is designed for public-facing services and lacks the internal certificate features needed. Option D overstates the constraint; a separate CA per environment is one valid approach, but Certificate Filters make it unnecessary, and Temporal Cloud supports both patterns.

questions.question: A team is designing a credential rotation procedure for Workers that connect to Temporal Cloud using mTLS certificates. Which step in the rotation sequence is most critical for achieving near-zero downtime?

choices.text: Revoking the old certificate immediately after generating the new one, so the two credentials never coexist and there is no window for credential confusion.
Switching all Workers to the new certificate in a single coordinated deployment before updating Temporal Cloud, ensuring the cluster always has exactly one valid credential set.
Completing the rotation entirely during a maintenance window so that no workflow traffic is processed while credentials are in transition.
Configuring Temporal Cloud to accept both the old and new credentials simultaneously before migrating Workers, so Workers can authenticate during the transition period.

questions.explanation: The dual-credential step is the key to zero-downtime rotation. The documented process is: generate new credentials → configure Temporal Cloud to accept both old and new → migrate Workers to the new credential → validate connectivity → then remove the old credential. If you remove the old credential before Workers have switched over (Option A), any Worker still using the old cert loses connectivity immediately, causing authentication failures and potentially stalling running workflows. Option B reverses the safe order — Workers would briefly have a credential that Temporal Cloud doesn't yet accept. Option C is operationally expensive and unnecessary; the dual-credential approach is specifically designed to avoid requiring a maintenance window.

questions.question: A Temporal Cloud workflow processes thousands of records over several days. The team notices their monthly bill is dominated by Active Storage costs. Which pattern most directly addresses this cost driver?

choices.text: Replace regular Activities with Local Activities so that back-to-back steps count as a single billable Action, reducing the Action component of the bill.
Reduce the namespace retention period from 30 days to 1 day so that closed Workflow Histories move out of storage faster after the workflow completes.
Call Continue-As-New periodically so the current execution closes and moves to cheaper Retained Storage, and a fresh execution resumes the remaining work.
Batch multiple UpsertSearchAttributes calls into a single call per workflow step, reducing the number of Actions consumed by attribute updates.

questions.explanation: Active Storage applies to open (running) Workflow Executions and their Event Histories, and it is significantly more expensive than Retained Storage. For long-running workflows that span days or weeks, calling Continue-As-New closes the current execution — moving its history to cheaper Retained Storage — and starts a fresh execution to continue processing. This is the primary lever for reducing Active Storage costs on long-running workflows. Option A targets Action costs, not Active Storage. Option B addresses Retained Storage (closed workflows), which the docs note typically represents only about 10% of a bill, and it has no effect on the Active Storage of a still-open workflow. Option D also reduces Actions, not storage, and the workflow described here has a storage-dominated cost profile.

questions.question: Your team rotates mTLS certificates for Temporal Cloud workers. During the last rotation, several workflows were interrupted for 4–6 minutes while workers failed to authenticate with the new certificates. You need to eliminate this downtime in the next rotation. Which process change fixes this?

choices.text: Generate the new certificate immediately before the old one expires, then redeploy all workers simultaneously with the new certificate to minimize the window where two certificates are active.
Switch to Let's Encrypt for automated certificate renewal so that expiration and rotation happen without manual intervention and workers never experience an authentication gap.
Use API keys instead of mTLS certificates because API keys support atomic swap rotation, whereas mTLS certificates always require a brief authentication gap during changeover.

Configure Temporal Cloud to accept both the old and new certificates simultaneously, migrate workers to the new certificate while the old one is still valid, confirm connectivity, then remove the old certificate.

questions.explanation: The documented zero-downtime rotation process requires a dual-credential window: generate new credentials, configure Temporal Cloud to accept both old and new, migrate workers gradually to the new credential, validate that all workers authenticate successfully, and only then remove the old credential. Doing a simultaneous cutover (A) is exactly the pattern that caused the 4–6 minute outage — there is a gap where old credentials are revoked before workers have restarted with new ones. Let's Encrypt (B) is explicitly not recommended for Temporal Cloud mTLS because it is designed for public-facing services and does not meet internal certificate requirements. The claim that API keys support atomic swap while mTLS never can (C) is false; both authentication methods support a dual-credential transition window using the same five-step process.

questions.question: A payment processing team consolidates five sequential Activities — validate input, call payment API, update database, send notification, generate receipt — into a single 'processOrder' Activity to reduce Temporal Cloud Action costs. Three weeks later, the on-call engineer reports that incidents take significantly longer to diagnose. Which specific operational capability did consolidation remove that is causing this?

choices.text: The Temporal scheduler can no longer distribute work across multiple workers because a single large Activity holds a task queue slot for longer, reducing overall throughput during incident windows.

Per-step visibility in the Temporal UI is lost, independent retry control for each step is eliminated, and workflow filtering by failure stage is no longer possible — all of which extend time to diagnose and resolve failures.

The consolidated Activity exceeds the 10-second local execution limit, causing it to be treated as a long-running Activity and triggering unexpected heartbeat timeouts that obscure the real failure cause.

Temporal Cloud billing changes from per-Activity Actions to per-workflow Actions after consolidation, which paradoxically increases costs because the workflow itself generates more Events during the longer single Activity execution.

questions.explanation: The documentation explicitly uses this exact scenario as an anti-pattern example. Merging five Activities into one 'processOrder' Activity means you lose per-step visibility in the Temporal UI (you cannot see which of the five steps failed), you lose independent retry control (a notification failure retries the entire flow including the payment API call), and you cannot filter workflows by failure stage. All three losses directly extend mean time to detect (MTTD) and mean time to repair (MTTR), which is why the on-call engineer is struggling. The scheduler throughput impact (A) is not how Temporal task queue scheduling works — Activity duration does not block other tasks on the queue. The 10-second local Activity limit (C) applies only to Local Activities, not regular Activities. The billing claim in (D) is incorrect; Actions are counted per Activity Execution, not restructured based on consolidation.

questions.question: Your organization runs dev and prod Temporal Cloud namespaces using certificates signed by the same internal CA. A developer accidentally connected a dev worker to the prod namespace last month, causing corrupted workflow state. Your security team wants a preventive control that does not require separate CAs. What is the correct Temporal-native approach?

choices.text: Rotate to API keys for all environments and disable mTLS entirely, because API keys support namespace-scoped roles whereas mTLS with a shared CA cannot distinguish between namespaces without separate CAs.

Issue certificates with a common name matching the namespace name, then configure Certificate Filters on each namespace to validate that the presented certificate's common name matches that namespace before allowing access.

Enforce network-level isolation by routing dev and prod through separate VPCs with distinct Temporal Cloud private link endpoints, so that workers in the dev VPC physically cannot reach the prod gRPC endpoint.

Generate unique intermediate CAs for dev and prod from the same root CA, which preserves the shared root for organizational trust while giving each environment a distinct signing chain that Temporal Cloud can differentiate.

questions.explanation: The documentation describes exactly this scenario: when multiple certificates are signed by the same CA and some are for production, you can give certificates a common name matching the namespace and then use Certificate Filters to prevent access to production environments. Certificate Filters are an additional validation layer applied to the client certificate during authentication, so even though the CA is shared, a dev certificate with common name 'my-namespace-dev' is rejected by a prod namespace configured to only accept 'my-namespace-prod'. Switching to API keys (A) would work for access control but the premise that mTLS with a shared CA 'cannot' be scoped is wrong — that is what Certificate Filters solve. Network isolation (C) is a valid defense-in-depth layer but is not a Temporal-native credential control and requires infrastructure changes. Intermediate CAs (D) are a valid PKI strategy but require a new CA issuance process, whereas the question asks for an approach that does not require separate CAs.

questions.question: A long-running order fulfillment workflow sleeps for 48 hours between steps while waiting for warehouse confirmation. Your Temporal Cloud Active Storage costs have grown 40% month-over-month as workflow volume increased. The workflow history per execution stays well under event limits. Which optimization most directly reduces Active Storage cost for this pattern?

choices.text: Implement a custom Data Converter with gzip compression on all workflow inputs and activity results to reduce the byte footprint of each event in the history during the sleep period.
Reduce the namespace retention period from 30 days to 7 days so that closed workflow histories are purged sooner, moving cost from the Active Storage tier to the cheaper Retained Storage tier faster.
Call Continue-As-New before the 48-hour sleep so the current execution closes and moves to the cheaper Retained Storage tier while waiting, then starts a fresh execution when work resumes.

Convert the 48-hour sleep into a scheduled child workflow that runs independently, because child workflows use a separate billing unit that does not contribute to the parent workflow's Active Storage footprint.

questions.explanation: Active Storage is significantly more expensive than Retained Storage and applies to open (running) workflows. A workflow sleeping for 48 hours is open and accumulating Active Storage costs for the entire wait period. The documentation explicitly calls out this pattern: for long-running workflows with extended sleep or wait periods, calling Continue-As-New before sleeping closes the current execution (moving it to cheaper Retained Storage) and starts fresh when work resumes. Compression (A) reduces payload byte size, which helps if payloads are large, but the question states history is well under event limits — the cost driver is duration in Active Storage, not payload size. Reducing retention (B) affects Retained Storage costs for closed workflows, not Active Storage for open ones — this does not help while the workflow is sleeping. Child workflows (D) cost 2 Actions vs 1 for an Activity and still run as open executions, so their histories also contribute to Active Storage; this does not move cost to a cheaper tier.

questions.question: Your organization runs Temporal Cloud with a single internal CA that signs certificates for both `dev` and `prod` namespaces. A security audit flags this as a risk: a misconfigured dev worker authenticated with a valid cert from the shared CA is able to connect to the prod namespace and poll task queues. You need to enforce namespace isolation without re-architecting your entire PKI. Which combination of controls directly addresses this threat within Temporal Cloud's access model?

choices.text: Rotate to separate CAs for dev and prod immediately; shared CAs are fundamentally incompatible with namespace isolation in Temporal Cloud, so Certificate Filters cannot compensate for the trust boundary they create.

Issue certs where the Common Name matches the target namespace name, then configure Certificate Filters on each namespace to reject certs whose CN does not match; this lets the shared CA remain while Temporal Cloud enforces per-namespace access at the TLS layer.

Switch all workers from mTLS to API keys and use service accounts with namespace-scoped roles, because Certificate Filters only apply to the Operations API and cannot restrict worker connectivity to a specific namespace.

Enable private link on the prod namespace so that only VPC-internal traffic reaches it, and place dev workers in a separate VPC without routing to prod; network segmentation alone is sufficient to replace certificate-level authorization.

questions.explanation: Temporal Cloud's Certificate Filters are an additional authorization layer applied on top of mTLS. After the TLS handshake validates that the client cert is signed by a trusted CA, Temporal Cloud can further inspect certificate fields—such as the Common Name—and reject connections that don't match configured filter criteria. By issuing certs with a CN equal to the namespace name (e.g., CN=prod for prod, CN=dev for dev) and setting a Certificate Filter on the prod namespace that requires CN=prod, you prevent any dev cert from successfully connecting to prod even though both certs share the same CA. This is explicitly supported in Temporal Cloud's authorization model and is the recommended workaround documented for shared-CA deployments. Option A is wrong: Certificate Filters exist precisely for this shared-CA scenario and the docs confirm this is a valid production pattern. Option C is wrong: Certificate Filters apply to worker connectivity, not just the Operations API; and switching to API keys would solve the problem differently but the claim about filter scope is factually incorrect. Option D is wrong: network-level segmentation is a defense-in-depth layer but does not replace credential-level authorization—a misconfigured route or future infra change would silently re-expose the namespace. The deeper design principle here is that Temporal Cloud treats mTLS as an authentication mechanism and Certificate Filters as a separate authorization mechanism; these are intentionally layered so you can add fine-grained access control without changing your PKI topology. In production, always validate that both layers are independently enforced.

questions.question: A platform engineering team at a high-scale fintech is reviewing Temporal Cloud costs. They discover that a payment reconciliation workflow runs thousands of concurrent executions per day, each sleeping for 23 hours between daily reconciliation windows. Storage costs have ballooned because active storage is billed at a much higher rate than retained storage. A junior engineer proposes removing all intermediate Activity results from the event history to shrink payload size. A senior engineer instead proposes a different architectural change. Which approach most directly and correctly targets the root cause of the elevated active storage cost?

choices.text: Implement a custom Data Converter with gzip compression on all payloads, which reduces the size of each event written to the history and therefore reduces active storage consumption across all concurrent executions during the sleep window.

Consolidate multiple Activities into a single 'reconcile' Activity to reduce the total event count per workflow execution, since fewer history events means smaller active storage footprint while the workflow is sleeping between windows.

Call Continue-As-New before the 23-hour sleep so the current execution closes (moving to cheaper retained storage) and a fresh execution starts when work resumes, eliminating the large window where thousands of executions sit in active storage.

Reduce the namespace retention period from 30 days to 1 day, which causes closed workflow histories to be purged faster and lowers the retained storage bill that accumulates from the high volume of daily completed executions.

questions.explanation: The root cause is that thousands of workflow executions are sitting in 'open' status during their 23-hour sleep, each accruing active storage charges at the more expensive active storage rate. Active storage applies to open (running) workflows and is significantly more expensive than retained storage. By calling Continue-As-New immediately before the long sleep, the current execution is closed—transitioning its history to the cheaper retained storage tier—and a new execution starts when work resumes. This directly targets the billing model mismatch: you pay active rates only for the short periods of actual work, not for the 23 hours of dormancy. Option A (compression) reduces payload size but does not change the billing tier; a compressed open workflow still accrues active storage charges on every byte. Option B (Activity consolidation) reduces event count, which slightly shrinks history size, but the workflow remains open for 23 hours at the active rate—this is also an observability anti-pattern the docs explicitly warn against. Option D (retention reduction) affects retained storage for closed workflows, not active storage for open ones; it targets the wrong cost component entirely. The broader design insight is that Temporal's two-tier storage pricing (active vs. retained) should be a first-class architectural consideration for long-running workflows with extended wait periods. Continue-As-New was designed partly for this reason: it is not just about event history length limits but also about giving engineers explicit control over the workflow lifecycle boundary, which in a consumption-pricing model directly maps to cost tier transitions. Engineers who conflate 'running' with 'doing work' will consistently over-pay on active storage.
